justification: All rubric criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken and unsuitable for security tokens (critical finding #1); HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended as replacement; timing attack via string equality identified (critical finding #2) with crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended; timestamp design flaw noted both as missing expiration enforcement and as generate/validate round-trip impossibility; safe_to_deploy is false.

observations.text: MD5 correctly classified as Critical with specific attack vectors (collision generators, NIST deprecation)
HMAC-SHA256 recommended with explanation of why HMAC over plain hash (length-extension attacks)
Timing attack explained with concrete complexity reduction (O(16^32) to O(16*32)) and crypto.timingSafeEqual fix provided
Timestamp flaw identified from two angles: no expiration enforcement and generate function discarding the timestamp
Length-extension attack on naive concatenation identified as a separate important issue — goes beyond rubric requirements
Production readiness section addresses migration complexity of fixing the hash algorithm
safe_to_deploy is false with clear rationale

agent_output_excerpt: {"summary":{"verdict":"BLOCK","one_liner":"Critical security flaws: broken hash algorithm, timing-attack-vulnerable comparison, and no token expiration enforcement make this unfit for production B2B auth."},"critical_findings":[{"title":"MD5 is cryptographically broken — tokens are forgeable","risk":"MD5 has known collision and preimage attacks. An attacker who intercepts one token can forge valid tokens for any partner. This is a B2B auth boundary — a forged token grants full API access to part
